impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense applies when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems not covered by the patent), defeating the nonstaple goods defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors benefit from clearer limits on patent holders' ability to leverage patent rights to compel purchase of unpatented products, opening tied product markets to competition.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk if they condition patent licenses on exclusive purchase of related but unpatented products, even in their core markets, when those products have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing emulated function calls to a model are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 at Alice step one, and whether damages may include sales to customers outside the United States.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting anomalous program executions (e.g., virus detection) are abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on foreign sales. The court further reversed a contempt order against Norton's counsel Quinn Emanuel and vacated related enhanced damages and attorneys' fees.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in cybersecurity patent cases benefit from expanded application of Alice to software-implemented detection methods, potentially invalidating broad patent claims in this space. The foreign sales damages ruling limits patent damages exposure for global technology companies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting software and cybersecurity patents face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges, and universities and other patent owners may see significant damages reductions if foreign sales are excluded from royalty calculations.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed on Netflix's cross-appeal, holding that GoTV's patents claiming server-side tailoring of content to a wireless device's capabilities are ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device capabilities, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix and overturning a $2.5 million jury verdict.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from clearer guidance that server-side content adaptation claims may be patent-ineligible, reducing exposure to infringement suits by non-practicing entities asserting broad streaming technology patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face increased risk that their patents will be invalidated under § 101, particularly where claims cover well-known concepts implemented via conventional technology.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled district court declaratory judgment action when it had previously missed the statutory deadline for staying the original action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) when it had already missed the deadline for staying the original action. The court prevented a party from circumventing the statutory time limits by dismissing and refiling its own district court action to reset the stay clock.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing ITC investigations retain access to district court declaratory judgment actions and cannot be blocked by opponents who manipulate filing timelines to obtain mandatory stays they previously forfeited.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the statutory deadline to request a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action cannot cure the deficiency by refiling their own declaratory judgment action to restart the clock.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims in a patent case involving coiled tubing technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (found in Global Tubing's favor) and Walker Process fraud (found in Tenaris's favor), holding that genuine disputes of material fact existed on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris failed to disclose prior CYMAX technology documents during patent prosecution and attempted monopolization of the coiled tubing market through patents obtained by fraud.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to find factual disputes on inequitable conduct, keeping alive defenses and counterclaims that can render patents unenforceable.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face prolonged and costly litigation where summary judgment on inequitable conduct is reversed, and Walker Process antitrust claims based on alleged patent fraud will proceed to trial.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices. The court upheld the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings, sustaining the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watches.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in medical device and wearable technology markets benefit from affirmation that the ITC provides an effective enforcement mechanism against large-scale importers of infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies face significant business disruption from ITC exclusion orders, potentially requiring product redesigns or removal of patented features to continue importing products into the United States.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy without allowing the plaintiff to cure the deficiencies.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft. The court found the district court abused its discretion by excluding the expert report without affording Exafer an opportunity to address deficiencies and by denying Exafer's motion to reopen discovery, then granting summary judgment based solely on the absence of a quantified remedy.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from the principle that courts should not summarily dispose of cases by excluding damages evidence without allowing reasonable opportunity to cure deficiencies in expert reports.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants relying on Daubert exclusion of damages experts as a path to summary judgment face a higher bar, as appellate courts may require district courts to provide plaintiffs opportunities to address expert report deficiencies.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a money-mandating obligation giving the Court of Federal Claims Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by applicants who were denied grants despite applying before other grantees.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses may proceed with claims against the United States for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that the statutory language directing SBA to 'award grants' in the order applications were received created a money-mandating obligation sufficient for Tucker Act jurisdiction, where the SBA processed applications out of order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied government grants under programs with mandatory award language have a viable path to recover damages when agencies fail to follow statutory disbursement procedures.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for improper administration of grant programs where Congress used mandatory language, potentially exposing the treasury to significant claims.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that PACER fees exceeded statutory authority by funding expenses beyond the cost of providing electronic court records access. The court upheld the settlement approval, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representative nonprofit organizations, rejecting objections from a pro se appellant.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are frequent PACER users will receive reimbursement for excessive fees, and the settlement establishes limits on the judiciary's authority to use PACER fees for unrelated expenses.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and trade competitors gain greater access to information in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, enhancing transparency and the ability to participate meaningfully in trade investigations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign producers and exporters face increased risk of disclosure of commercially sensitive business information submitted in response to ITC questionnaires, potentially deterring full cooperation with ITC investigations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in finding patent claims indefinite under 35 U.S.C. § 112 based on means-plus-function limitations where the specification disclosed the corresponding structure.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of a patent directed to crop harvester header height control were invalid as indefinite. The court found that the specification adequately disclosed the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation, and remanded for further proceedings on the remaining issues in the case.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's recognition that means-plus-function claims are not automatically indefinite when the specification discloses conventional structures like controllers and hydraulic systems, even without exhaustive detail.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims on indefiniteness grounds when the specification identifies the corresponding structure, even if described at a general level.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution must be issued through notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary non-institution of IPR proceedings are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements. The court reasoned that the instructions do not bind the Director and merely guide the Board's exercise of delegated discretionary authority that is itself insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains flexibility to adjust IPR institution policies without the burden of formal rulemaking, allowing responsive management of patent review proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty from PTO discretionary denial policies that can be changed without public notice and comment, limiting their ability to anticipate and plan for patent challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed claims of a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, specifically the meaning of 'encryption information' in the context of adaptive bitrate streaming.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The ruling affects interpretation of key claim terms in streaming and DRM technology patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies benefit from clearer claim construction standards for encryption and DRM-related patents, potentially narrowing the scope of asserted claims in this commercially significant area.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming media encryption space face risk that narrower claim constructions may make their patents more vulnerable to obviousness challenges in IPR proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Fortress Iron, LP v. Digger Specialties, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent is invalid for omission of a coinventor when the patent holder acknowledges the coinventorship but cannot locate the omitted inventor to obtain consent for correction under 35 U.S.C. § 256.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that patents naming only two of four actual coinventors were invalid where the patent holder could not locate one omitted coinventor to add him through the § 256 correction process. The court also affirmed denial of the patent holder's motion to judicially correct inventorship under § 256(b), holding that inability to locate a coinventor does not excuse the inventorship deficiency.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from a strong inventorship defense, as patent holders bear the burden of ensuring all inventors are named and cannot enforce patents with known inventorship defects.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies collaborating with foreign manufacturers on inventions face serious risk that failure to track and maintain contact with all contributors could result in patent invalidity, even where the omission is acknowledged and partially corrected.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 who receive Title 38 base pay are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and related regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the dismissal of claims by IHS registered nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work. The court held that nurses paid under Title 38's special salary provisions may also be entitled to Title 38's enhanced compensation rates for premium pay, and remanded for further proceedings on backpay claims under 5 U.S.C. § 5596.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potentially significant backpay liability for nurses and other healthcare workers who may have been underpaid for premium work during the COVID-19 pandemic and beyond, with potential class-wide implications.
